channel.title: ZQUAS Articles
channel.description: Analysis and perspective on compliance technology, regulatory architecture, and the future of cross-institutional financial crime detection.
channel.copyright: (c) ZQUAS. Bounded Compliance on an Unbounded Platform.

item.title: Why We Built Our Own AI Inference Engine
item.description: ChatGPT runs on one. Llama runs on one. We wrote our own. Why a financial crime company built its own GPU inference engine in C++23 and CUDA, and why regulated AI decisions need a different stack from the one a chatbot needs.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: Beyond Banking: Cross-Sector Federated Detection for Financial Crime, Telecommunications Fraud, and Digital Asset Compliance
item.description: A single detection engine that correlates risk signals across banks, telecommunications operators, and digital asset platforms without sharing raw data between institutions. The same code, the same cryptographic protocols, the same GPU-native detection pipeline, applied to every regulated sector where financial crime operates.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: What Article 75 Was Afraid to Permit
item.description: AMLR Article 75 restricts cross-institutional data sharing to high-risk customers. The criminals who are hardest to catch are the ones who stay low-risk at every bank. Multi-Party Computation resolves the paradox: full customer base federation without sharing a single byte of personal data. The evidence, the legal argument, and the path to a pilot.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: From Edge to Federation: A Unified Architecture for Real-Time Financial Crime Detection
item.description: A single detection engine that replaces overnight batch processing with continuous real-time monitoring. Three layers, one binary. Institutional real-time monitoring, cross-bank federation without data sharing, and regulatory interface. Three use cases, the ESG case, and the regulatory alignment map.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: The AML Industry Has Never Proven Detection Works. We Built a Way to Measure It.
item.description: Every AML vendor claims high detection rates. Not a single one can prove it. The ZQUAS Financial Crime Network Simulator generates complete financial ecosystems at production scale, embeds criminal networks of known structure and difficulty, and measures detection accuracy with mathematical precision.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: What Does Real-Time Actually Mean?
item.description: Every transaction monitoring vendor claims real-time. The term has three distinct meanings with fundamentally different implications for detection capability. A framework for evaluating what real-time actually delivers.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: EU AI Act and AML: Is Your Transaction Monitoring System High-Risk?
item.description: The EU AI Act is the first comprehensive AI regulation in the world. Most AML transaction monitoring systems are excluded from high-risk classification. But the profiling exception creates an important grey area that banks using AI-based customer risk scoring cannot ignore.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: The 16-Month Window: Why Banks Must Act Before July 2027
item.description: AMLR Article 75 applies on July 10, 2027. Cross-institutional monitoring infrastructure takes 18 to 24 months to build. Banks that begin vendor evaluation in Q1 2026 can be ready. Banks that wait until 2027 cannot.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: UK Banks Can Share AML Data Today: ECCTA Sections 188-189
item.description: While the EU waits until July 2027 for AMLR Article 75, the UK has already acted. ECCTA Sections 188 and 189 have been in force since January 2024. UK banks have the legal basis to implement collaborative AML detection now.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: From TMNL to Article 75: The Netherlands' Path to Collaborative AML
item.description: In 2019, five Dutch banks created the most ambitious collaborative AML project in Europe. By 2025, it was being wound down. The technology was right. The legal framework was not ready. AMLR Article 75 changes that.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: MPC and GDPR: What the EDPB Actually Says
item.description: The European Data Protection Board has identified multi-party computation as an accountability tool for cross-border data processing. Their position is more specific and more supportive than most compliance teams realise. Here is what it says and what it means for collaborative AML.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: What AMLR Article 75 Means for Dutch Banks
item.description: The Netherlands tried collaborative AML detection. It failed because the law was not ready. AMLR Article 75 creates an explicit legal basis for bank-to-bank information sharing. Dutch banks now have 16 months to build what TMNL could not.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: Why Cross-Institutional AML Detection Requires a New Architecture
item.description: Joint transaction monitoring initiatives have stalled across Europe. The core assumption — that centralising data is the path to better detection — is architecturally flawed. Privacy-preserving computation offers a fundamentally different approach.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: What Running a Multi-Bank Monitoring Network Actually Looks Like
item.description: The architecture papers write themselves. The hard part is operations. How do banks join? Who sets the rules? What happens when one bank's policy change affects another bank's alerts? These are the questions that determine whether a network works or collapses.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: The EUR 200 Billion Compliance Market Nobody Has Disrupted
item.description: Financial institutions spend more on compliance than on almost any other technology category. The tooling hasn't fundamentally changed in 15 years. Here's why, and what's about to break that open.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: Privacy-Preserving Detection Under AMLR
item.description: The EU AMLR creates a catch-22 for cross-institutional detection. You can only share data about customers you've already identified as high-risk. But you need shared data to identify them in the first place. There is a way through this.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: Batch Processing Is a Liability on Real-Time Payment Rails
item.description: Your payment infrastructure settles in seconds. Your AML monitoring runs overnight. That gap is where both criminals and regulators will find you.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: The Real Cost of 95% False Positives
item.description: Everyone in compliance knows the number. Ninety-five percent of AML alerts are false positives. But the real damage isn't in the alerts themselves. It's in everything that happens around them.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: From Trust-Based Audit to Cryptographic Verification
item.description: Current supervisory models depend on banks accurately reporting how their systems work. Cryptographic attestation replaces that trust with mathematical proof. Here's what changes for supervisors.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: Supervising AI in AML: What Article 12 Actually Requires
item.description: The EU AI Act may classify AI-based AML risk profiling as high-risk. Article 12 demands record-keeping. But what does technically adequate record-keeping look like when a system makes millions of decisions per day?
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: What Your AML Vendor Can't Prove
item.description: Your monitoring system generates alerts, case files, and reports. But can it cryptographically prove that a specific policy was applied to specific data at a specific time? That question is coming.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: Same Data, Same Rules, Different Verdict: The Determinism Problem in AML
item.description: Run the same transaction through the same monitoring system tomorrow and you might get a different result. That's not a bug. It's an architecture problem. And regulators are starting to notice.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: AMLR 2027: Why Every Bank in Europe Faces a Hard Rebuild Deadline
item.description: This isn't a regulatory update banks can absorb with a policy tweak. AMLR requires architectural changes that legacy monitoring systems weren't designed for. The deadline is mid-2027. The rebuild starts now.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: Why GPU-Native Compliance Is a Moat, Not a Feature
item.description: Competitors can copy a feature in a sprint. They can't replicate a GPU-native architecture with privacy-preserving MPC and cryptographic attestation in under three years. Here's why the gap is structural.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: Land, Expand, Network: How Sovereign Compliance Scales
item.description: Start with one bank. Add cross-institutional detection as the federation grows. Each new participant makes the system better for everyone. The business model has network effects built into the architecture.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: Why Compliance Tech Hasn't Been Disrupted Yet, and What Changes That
item.description: The most conservative buyers in the most regulated industry, using tools that haven't changed in 15 years. There are specific reasons this market has resisted disruption. Those reasons are expiring.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: If Your AI Compliance System Can't Attack Itself, How Do You Know It Works?
item.description: The EU AI Act requires continuous risk management for high-risk AI systems. Most AML vendors test against historical data. That tells you how the system performed in the past. It says nothing about how it will handle attacks it hasn't seen yet.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: Reactive Compliance Is a Losing Strategy. Here's What Comes After.
item.description: Every compliance system on the market tells you what already happened. What if the system could tell you what's about to go wrong, before it does? That's not a hypothetical anymore.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: Why a Governance Runtime Is Worth More Than a Monitoring Tool
item.description: The difference between a compliance monitoring tool and a governance runtime is the difference between a dashcam and an autopilot. One records what happened. The other prevents what shouldn't.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: Your New Compliance System Won't Work If It Can't Talk to Your Old One
item.description: The most common reason compliance technology projects fail isn't the technology. It's integration. If the new system can't ingest from your existing payment rails and export to your existing SIEM, it's dead on arrival.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier

item.title: Transaction Monitoring Netherlands: What Went Wrong, What Comes Next
item.description: The most ambitious cross-institutional AML initiative in Europe was shut down because its architecture couldn't be reconciled with privacy law. A complete analysis of what happened, why it failed, and what the alternative architecture looks like.
item.dc:creator: Danny de Gier
